enrichment.short_description: Boxbot is a supply chain automation company based in Alameda, California, that builds AI-driven storage and retrieval systems for parcel logistics. Founded in 2016 by Austin Oehlerking and Mark Godwin, the company originally set out to build autonomous last-mile delivery trucks, but pivoted in 2023 after discovering stronger customer demand for its box-handling robotics technology. Boxbot's core product, the Boxbot Operating System (BOS), turns ordinary conveyors into three-dimensional, AI-optimized storage systems using proprietary trays and a modular, gantry-based architecture that can reach up to 25 feet in height. The system is designed to roughly double storage density versus static shelving while maintaining 99.9 percent uptime, handling items from small padded envelopes up to 75-pound parcels at throughput rates from 250 to over 4,000 pieces per hour. Boxbot sells to last-mile parcel carriers, third-party logistics providers, airports and air cargo operators, industrial facilities, and retailers that need dense, dynamic buffering and sortation without expanding their physical footprint. The company has raised roughly 29.5 million dollars in venture funding, including a 12 million dollar Series A led by Playground Global with participation from Maersk Growth and Toyota Ventures.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Boxbot is a robotics and supply-chain automation company headquartered in Alameda, California. It was founded in 2016 by Austin Oehlerking and Mark Godwin, initially as a stealth autonomous last-mile delivery truck startup competing in the self-driving van space. During customer conversations, the team found that prospects were far more excited about a secondary box-handling and storage robot the company had built than about the self-driving trucks themselves. In 2023 Boxbot formally pivoted around that signal, becoming a company focused entirely on robotic storage and retrieval for parcels, and announced a 12 million dollar Series A led by Playground Global with participation from Maersk Growth, Toyota Ventures, and Pear Ventures, bringing total disclosed funding to roughly 29.5 million dollars. Boxbot's flagship offering is the Boxbot Operating System (BOS), an AI software layer paired with a modular, gantry-based hardware system that attaches to standard conveyor infrastructure. Rather than requiring a fully custom automated warehouse, Boxbot's approach retrofits the humble conveyor belt into a three-dimensional storage system: proprietary trays carry individual parcels up into a vertical gantry structure that can reach up to 25 feet, effectively turning flat floor space into dense, dynamically managed storage. The company claims roughly double the storage density of static shelving, 99.9 percent uptime, and full-stack sensor fusion for fault avoidance and monitoring. On the throughput side, Boxbot's systems are built to handle a wide range of parcel sizes, from about 6 by 6 by 1 inches and 1 pound up to 35 by 24 by 24 inches and 75 pounds, at rates from 250 to more than 4,000 pieces per hour depending on configuration. Target customers include last-mile parcel carriers looking to buffer and stage packages at delivery stations, third-party logistics (3PL) operators needing cross-dock buffering, airports and air cargo/bonded storage facilities, industrial sites, and retailers that want automated counter retrieval for click-and-collect or returns. Because Boxbot sells a combined hardware-and-software system rather than a subscription SaaS product, it does not publish pricing; deployments are quoted and engineered per facility, which is typical for industrial robotics vendors. Boxbot is a relatively young company in its current form - its parcel-storage product line is only a few years old following the 2023 pivot - so its track record of large-scale, multi-year deployments is still being built, and it competes against larger, more established players such as GreyOrange, Addverb, and Unbox Robotics in the broader warehouse automation category. Prospective buyers evaluating Boxbot should expect an enterprise sales process, facility assessment, and integration planning rather than a self-serve signup, which is standard for this category of physical automation technology.

long_form_blocks.heading: From Autonomous Delivery Trucks to Robotic Parcel Storage
paragraphs: Boxbot did not start out building storage robots. When Austin Oehlerking and Mark Godwin founded the company in 2016, the plan was to build autonomous last-mile delivery trucks for a Bay Area self-driving-vehicle market that, at the time, looked poised for rapid growth. Like many startups in that wave, Boxbot built real technology and ran pilots, but the vehicle side of the business never found the traction the founders expected.

What did find traction was a side project: a robotic system for storing and retrieving packages that the team had built to support its delivery operations. Customers who saw early demos consistently asked more questions about the box-handling robot than about the self-driving van. Rather than force a product that wasn't resonating, Boxbot leaned into what the market was actually telling it, and in 2023 the company completed a full pivot to become a robotic storage and sortation company, raising a 12 million dollar Series A led by Playground Global to fund the transition.

long_form_blocks.heading: How the Boxbot Operating System Works
paragraphs: Boxbot's core insight is that most warehouses and delivery stations already have an underused piece of infrastructure: the conveyor belt. Conveyors move packages efficiently in two dimensions but do almost nothing to help with storage, buffering, or retrieval. The Boxbot Operating System (BOS) pairs proprietary storage trays with a modular, gantry-based structure - up to 25 feet tall - that attaches to standard conveyors and turns that flat, one-directional motion into dense, three-dimensional, addressable storage.

BOS uses AI to make real-time decisions about where to place and retrieve each item, aiming to maximize density (Boxbot claims roughly 2x versus static shelving) while keeping retrieval times low. The system is designed to handle a wide size and weight range - from small envelopes up to 75-pound parcels - at throughput rates that scale from a few hundred to several thousand pieces per hour, with full-stack sensor fusion intended to catch faults before they cause downtime and support a 99.9 percent uptime target.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Uses Boxbot and How It's Positioned
paragraphs: Boxbot's core customers are parcel carriers that need to buffer and stage packages at last-mile delivery stations, third-party logistics providers running cross-dock operations, airports and air cargo facilities that require secure bonded storage, and retailers that want automated retrieval for counter pickup, click-and-collect, or returns processing. In each of these cases, the pitch is the same: get significantly more storage capacity and throughput out of an existing footprint rather than leasing or building additional warehouse space.

As an enterprise robotics vendor, Boxbot does not publish self-serve pricing; deployments are engineered and quoted per site, and the company works with an enterprise sales process typical of industrial automation. Boxbot competes with larger, more established players in the broader warehouse and parcel-automation space, including GreyOrange, Addverb, and Unbox Robotics, and its relatively recent pivot (2023) means its long-term deployment track record is still developing compared to some competitors that have been selling automation systems for longer.

enrichment.short_description: BoxyHQ is an open-source enterprise identity and security infrastructure company founded in August 2021 by Deepak Prabhakara and Carlos Samame, originally headquartered in London, United Kingdom. It built a suite of self-hostable, developer-first building blocks - most notably SAML Jackson for SAML and OIDC single sign-on, a SCIM 2.0 Directory Sync service for automated user provisioning and deprovisioning, and Retraced for audit logging - that let SaaS companies add enterprise-ready authentication and compliance features without building SSO plumbing from scratch. The tools are offered under open-source licenses for self-hosting, with a hosted or enterprise tier for teams that want a managed service, SLAs, and support. In May 2025, identity infrastructure company Ory acquired BoxyHQ, and BoxyHQ's product line has since been rebranded and continued as Ory Polis (SSO and Directory Sync) alongside Ory's broader identity platform, though the original open-source repositories remain available and are still maintained under the Ory organization.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: BoxyHQ built open-source SAML SSO, SCIM Directory Sync, and audit logging for SaaS teams. See what it offers and how it became Ory Polis after Ory's 2025 acquisition.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BoxyHQ was founded in August 2021 by Deepak Prabhakara and Carlos Samame with a focused mission: help SaaS startups add the enterprise-grade authentication and compliance features that large customers demand, without each team having to build SAML SSO, directory sync, and audit logging from scratch. Its first and best-known product, SAML Jackson, wraps the notoriously fiddly SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect protocols behind a simple OAuth 2.0-style flow, so developers can integrate enterprise SSO with a small amount of code rather than months of protocol work. On top of SSO, BoxyHQ built a SCIM 2.0-based Directory Sync service that lets enterprise customers automatically provision and deprovision user accounts and keep group membership in sync with their own identity provider in real time, and Retraced, an audit-log product that records and makes searchable the security-relevant events happening inside a customer's application. All three products could be run as a standalone self-hosted service or embedded via an npm library, and BoxyHQ maintained an admin portal for managing SSO, Directory Sync, and Audit Logs from a single interface, plus a Next.js-based 'SaaS Starter Kit' boilerplate to help teams bootstrap enterprise-ready applications faster. The open-source model meant BoxyHQ's code was broadly used and referenced across the developer community, with integrations into popular frameworks and auth libraries such as NextAuth.js/Auth.js. In May 2025, Ory - a larger identity and access management company - acquired BoxyHQ, describing the deal as a way to help enterprises modernize identity management and move away from monolithic identity providers. Following the acquisition, BoxyHQ's flagship SSO and Directory Sync products were rebranded as Ory Polis, and the GitHub organization now carries a 'BoxyHQ (acquired by Ory)' label, with active development continuing under the Ory brand (for example, the ory/polis repository). Retraced and other BoxyHQ-branded tools have similarly been folded into Ory's product and blog content. For a directory listing in 2026, it's important to be precise: 'BoxyHQ' as an independent company effectively no longer exists as a separate go-to-market entity - the technology, team, and roadmap now live inside Ory. Teams evaluating this technology today will most likely interact with it as Ory Polis, Ory Directory Sync, or a related Ory product, even though the open-source repositories and much of the developer-facing branding still carry the BoxyHQ name in places. Anyone researching 'BoxyHQ' should treat it as the historical and technical foundation of what is now part of Ory's identity platform rather than as an actively marketed standalone product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What BoxyHQ Built: Enterprise-Ready Auth as a Building Block
paragraphs: BoxyHQ's core thesis was that most SaaS companies eventually hit the same wall: a large enterprise prospect asks for SAML SSO, SCIM-based user provisioning, or detailed audit logs, and the engineering team has to drop everything to build protocol-level plumbing that has nothing to do with the product's actual value proposition. BoxyHQ's answer was to package that plumbing as open-source, embeddable building blocks - SAML Jackson for SSO, a SCIM 2.0 Directory Sync service, and Retraced for audit logging - so teams could add 'enterprise readiness' in days rather than months.

Because the products were open source, teams could self-host them for free and audit the code directly, which mattered for security-conscious buyers. BoxyHQ also offered a managed/enterprise tier for teams that preferred a hosted service with SLAs and support, and it shipped integrations and documentation for popular frameworks including Next.js, Express, and NextAuth.js/Auth.js, along with a SaaS Starter Kit to help new products launch with enterprise features baked in from day one.

long_form_blocks.heading: The 2025 Ory Acquisition and the Move to Ory Polis
paragraphs: In May 2025, Ory - an identity infrastructure company with a broader platform spanning authentication, authorization, and identity management - acquired BoxyHQ. The stated rationale was to help enterprises modernize identity management and reduce dependence on large, monolithic identity providers, folding BoxyHQ's lightweight, developer-first SSO and directory sync tools into Ory's stack.

Following the deal, BoxyHQ's flagship SAML Jackson and Directory Sync products were rebranded as Ory Polis, and BoxyHQ's GitHub organization now displays an 'acquired by Ory' label. Ory has continued to maintain and ship updates to the underlying open-source project (now living at ory/polis), while folding Retraced-style audit logging concepts into its broader security and compliance messaging. For research and directory purposes, this means 'BoxyHQ' is best understood today as the origin story and technical foundation for a set of identity tools that now operate under the Ory brand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Where This Technology Fits for SaaS Teams
paragraphs: Whether approached as BoxyHQ's original open-source repositories or as Ory Polis today, the underlying value proposition is unchanged: it gives engineering teams a fast, self-hostable or managed path to enterprise SSO, directory sync, and audit logging, which are frequently prerequisites for closing larger B2B deals. This makes it most relevant to SaaS companies moving upmarket toward enterprise customers who require SAML/OIDC login and automated user lifecycle management.

Teams evaluating this technology should go in expecting some documentation and branding to be mid-migration between the BoxyHQ and Ory names, and should confirm current product names, repositories, and support channels directly with Ory rather than assuming BoxyHQ still operates as an independent vendor.

enrichment.short_description: Bradfield School of Computer Science was a San Francisco-based, in-person and live-online school that taught practicing software engineers the foundational computer science they typically missed - algorithms and data structures, computer architecture, networking, operating systems, databases, and distributed systems - through small, discussion-based classes rather than video lectures. Founded and run by instructor Oz Nova starting around the mid-2010s, Bradfield built a reputation in the Bay Area tech community for rigorous, textbook-and-paper-driven courses aimed at mid-career engineers who wanted to deepen their CS fundamentals rather than learn a new framework. Over roughly eight years of operation the school taught more than 1,000 students, offering both standalone courses (such as Computer Architecture, Deep Learning for Engineers, and Engineering Leadership) and a flagship 12-month, part-time live-online CS fundamentals program. In 2023, Nova put Bradfield's live cohort courses on indefinite hiatus once the final cohorts finished, redirecting his effort into CS Primer, a self-paced platform designed to make the best parts of Bradfield's curriculum more affordable and accessible. Bradfield is not currently enrolling new live cohorts.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Bradfield School of Computer Science taught mid-career engineers CS fundamentals in San Francisco. Learn what it offered, why it's on hiatus, and where its curriculum lives now.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Bradfield School of Computer Science was an unusual entrant in the tech-education landscape: rather than teaching people how to code or use a specific framework, it taught practicing software engineers the underlying computer science concepts - algorithms and data structures, computer architecture, networking, operating systems, databases, and distributed systems - that many self-taught or bootcamp-trained engineers never formally studied. The school was founded and run out of San Francisco by instructor Oz Nova starting around 2015, and over roughly eight years it built a strong reputation within the Bay Area engineering community for its rigorous, discussion-based teaching style. Classes were intentionally small and combined tutorials, hands-on programming exercises, textbook study, and reading of primary research papers, a format closer to a graduate seminar than a coding bootcamp or a MOOC. Bradfield's course catalog included both standalone offerings - for example, Computer Architecture, Deep Learning for Engineers (taught by Dr. Brian Spiering), and an Engineering Leadership course (taught by Xavier Shay) - and its flagship program, a 12-month, part-time, live-online curriculum designed for mid-career software engineers who wanted to accelerate their technical depth without leaving their jobs. Students could historically attend in person in San Francisco or join live online, and over its run the school taught more than 1,000 students. In 2023, after eight years of running live cohorts, Oz Nova decided to put Bradfield's live-course offerings on indefinite hiatus once the then-current cohorts finished. In his own account, the decision wasn't driven by the school failing - curriculum and brand recognition had both improved year over year - but by a personal calculation about where he could have the most impact with his limited time and energy. Rather than continuing to scale a live, instructor-intensive model, Nova redirected his focus to CS Primer, a self-paced platform intended to preserve the best parts of the Bradfield curriculum and philosophy while making it more affordable and accessible to a much wider, asynchronous audience. As of the time of this research, Bradfield's website exists in a largely archival state, and the school is not accepting new live-cohort enrollments; anyone interested in the same style of rigorous computer science education is directed toward CS Primer instead. Because there are no active pricing plans or intake process, this profile should be read as an accurate historical and reference entry for the school's teaching philosophy and legacy rather than a live product listing that visitors can currently sign up for.

long_form_blocks.heading: A Different Kind of Coding Education: CS Fundamentals for Working Engineers
paragraphs: Most tech-education products in the 2010s and 2020s fell into one of two camps: bootcamps that taught people to code quickly enough to get a first job, or massive open online courses that let anyone watch pre-recorded lectures at their own pace. Bradfield School of Computer Science occupied a different niche entirely. Its students were typically already working software engineers - often self-taught or bootcamp graduates - who had learned to build things but had never formally studied the computer science underneath their day-to-day work: how algorithms are analyzed, how computers and networks actually function, how databases are built, how distributed systems fail.

Bradfield's answer was a curriculum built around textbooks, primary research papers, and small-group programming exercises, taught by instructors with real depth in narrow subjects rather than generalist course creators. Classes were intentionally kept small so that discussion and problem-solving stayed central, in contrast to the passive-viewing model of most online courses. This approach won the school a loyal following and strong word-of-mouth reputation among engineers in the Bay Area and beyond, even though it never tried to scale to the size of a mass-market bootcamp or MOOC platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Course Formats: Standalone Classes and a 12-Month Flagship Program
paragraphs: Over its roughly eight years of operation, Bradfield offered two main formats. Standalone courses covered a specific, deep topic over a shorter period - examples include Computer Architecture, Deep Learning for Engineers (taught by Dr. Brian Spiering), and an Engineering Leadership course taught by Xavier Shay - and were aimed at engineers who wanted to fill a specific gap in their knowledge without committing to a long program.

The flagship offering was a 12-month, part-time, live-online curriculum built for mid-career engineers who wanted a structured path through core computer science over the course of a year, without leaving their jobs. Students could historically attend classes in person in San Francisco or participate live online, and across its run the school reports having taught more than 1,000 students in total.

long_form_blocks.heading: The 2023 Hiatus and the Move to CS Primer
paragraphs: In 2023, founder Oz Nova put Bradfield's live cohort courses on indefinite hiatus once the final active cohorts had graduated. By his own account, the decision wasn't a response to the school struggling - curriculum quality and brand recognition had both grown steadily - but a personal choice about where to direct his time and energy going forward, given that running a live, instructor-driven school at that level of quality is inherently time- and attention-intensive.

In place of new live cohorts, Nova has focused on CS Primer, a self-paced platform intended to carry forward the philosophy and much of the content of Bradfield's courses in a more affordable, asynchronous format that can reach a wider audience than a live, San Francisco-centered school ever could. As of this writing, Bradfield's own site functions mainly as an archive and point of contact rather than an active enrollment page, and prospective students interested in Bradfield's teaching approach are directed to CS Primer instead.

enrichment.short_description: 'Braid' refers to the open protocol and research project hosted at braid.org, which extends HTTP from a state-transfer protocol into a full state-synchronization protocol. Led by Michael Toomim and developed collaboratively through an open working group and the IETF standards process (draft-toomim-httpbis-braid-http), Braid adds versioning, subscriptions (real-time updates on GET requests), patches (fine-grained updates via Range Requests), and merge-types (so servers can declare how conflicting edits should be resolved, for example via CRDTs or operational transforms) to ordinary HTTP. The goal is to let any web resource stay synchronized across multiple clients, servers, and proxies, enabling collaborative, offline-capable, local-first, and peer-to-peer web applications without every team having to build a bespoke real-time sync layer. Note that 'Braid' is an ambiguous name in software: this profile covers the braid.org state-synchronization protocol specifically, not GitHub's now-defunct 'git braid' submodule tool, not unrelated chat or messaging products sometimes called Braid, and not any commercial company of the same name. Braid is a non-commercial, open, community-maintained specification and reference-implementation ecosystem rather than a SaaS product or company.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Braid extends HTTP into a state synchronization protocol for real-time, local-first web apps. Learn what braid.org is, how it works, and how it differs from other tools named Braid.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Braid, as hosted at braid.org, is not a company or a commercial SaaS product - it is an open protocol and research project focused on a single, hard problem: HTTP was designed to transfer a snapshot of a resource's state from server to client, but the modern web increasingly needs resources to stay synchronized in real time across many clients, servers, and proxies, with support for concurrent edits, offline use, and peer-to-peer topologies. Rather than asking every team to invent a bespoke real-time sync layer on top of HTTP (as most collaborative apps do today, using WebSockets, custom polling, or proprietary backend services), Braid proposes extending HTTP itself with a small number of well-defined additions. The core specification, often referred to as Braid-HTTP and formalized as an IETF Internet-Draft (draft-toomim-httpbis-braid-http), adds four capabilities: versioning of HTTP resources, so clients and servers can refer to specific states of a resource over time; subscriptions on GET requests, so a client can ask to be notified of updates rather than polling; patches delivered through an extension of HTTP's existing Range Request mechanism, allowing fine-grained partial updates instead of re-sending an entire resource; and merge-types, which let a resource declare how simultaneous, conflicting writes should be reconciled - for example using a CRDT (conflict-free replicated data type) or an operational transform algorithm. Put together, these four pieces let an ordinary web resource behave like a shared, live document: any client can read it, subscribe to changes, write to it concurrently with other clients, and trust that the server (or peer) will merge those changes according to a well-defined algorithm, all while remaining compatible with existing HTTP infrastructure like caches and proxies. Braid is led by Michael Toomim and developed through an open, transparent process: contributors meet biweekly, discuss on public mailing lists and GitHub, and move the specification forward using the IETF's 'rough consensus' model rather than a single vendor's roadmap. Around the core protocol, an ecosystem of reference implementations and demo applications has grown up, including Braid-HTTP server/client libraries, Braid-Text (a CRDT-based collaborative text implementation), a Braid-Chrome browser extension, Braidfs (a filesystem-level sync tool), and small demonstration apps such as Peery View, the peer-to-peer chat app Quiet, the collaborative code-editing tool Ethersync, and Braidmail. Related open-source sync engines and CRDT libraries in the surrounding ecosystem - such as Diamond Types, Sync9, CR-SQLite, Redwood, and Statebus - are frequently discussed alongside Braid, though they are separate projects with their own maintainers. Because it is an open specification rather than a product, Braid has no pricing, account system, or company behind it; anyone can implement it, and there is no vendor lock-in. At the same time, it remains an evolving Internet-Draft rather than a finalized, universally adopted standard, so production use requires real engineering investment and a tolerance for a still-maturing spec. Given how common the name 'Braid' is in software - it has also been used for GitHub's discontinued Git submodule tool and for various unrelated messaging or collaboration products - anyone researching this entry should confirm they mean the braid.org state-synchronization protocol specifically.

long_form_blocks.heading: The Problem Braid Is Trying to Solve
paragraphs: HTTP, as originally designed, is a state-transfer protocol: a client asks for a resource, the server sends back a snapshot, and that's the end of the interaction until the client asks again. This model works well for static documents but breaks down for the collaborative, real-time applications that define much of today's web - shared documents, live dashboards, multiplayer tools, chat apps - all of which need a resource's state to stay synchronized across many clients as it changes.

In practice, most teams solve this by bolting a separate real-time layer onto HTTP: WebSockets, long polling, or a proprietary backend service that handles sync, conflict resolution, and offline support outside the normal request/response model. Braid's premise is that this pattern is repeated so often, and solved so inconsistently, that it belongs in the protocol layer itself, the same way HTTP eventually absorbed caching, compression, and range requests as first-class features rather than leaving every application to reinvent them.

long_form_blocks.heading: How Braid Extends HTTP
paragraphs: Braid-HTTP, the core specification developed as an IETF Internet-Draft under Michael Toomim's leadership, adds four building blocks to HTTP. Versioning lets resources be referenced at specific points in time rather than only as their current snapshot. Subscriptions extend the familiar GET request so a client can ask to be pushed updates as a resource changes, instead of polling. Patches extend HTTP's existing Range Request mechanism so that updates can be sent as small, targeted diffs instead of full resource re-transfers. And merge-types let a resource declare, in a standard way, how the server or peers should reconcile simultaneous conflicting writes - for instance by applying a CRDT or an operational transform.

Together, these four extensions are designed to let any HTTP resource become synchronizable, supporting concurrent multi-writer edits, offline operation, and peer-to-peer replication, while staying backwards-compatible with existing HTTP infrastructure such as browsers, caches, and proxies. The project also maintains a growing set of reference implementations and demo applications - including Braid-Text, Braidfs, a Braid-Chrome extension, and apps like Quiet and Ethersync - that put these ideas into practice and serve as proof that the approach works outside of pure specification documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who's Behind Braid, and What Adopting It Actually Means
paragraphs: Braid is not a company and has no commercial owner. It is organized as an open working group that meets regularly (historically biweekly via video call), discusses on public mailing lists and GitHub, and advances its specification through the IETF's standards process, which relies on public drafts and rough consensus rather than a single vendor's product roadmap. This makes Braid closer in spirit to protocols like WebDAV or Server-Sent Events than to a commercial developer platform.

For teams evaluating Braid, the practical implication is that there's no vendor to buy from, no SLA, and no pricing - only an open specification, a set of reference implementations, and a community to engage with. That's an advantage for teams that want to avoid lock-in and are comfortable contributing to or adapting early-stage tooling, and a limitation for teams that need a polished, supported, drop-in product today. Because Braid is still an evolving Internet-Draft rather than a ratified web standard, adopting it in production requires real engineering investment and a willingness to track an actively changing specification.

faqs.question: What is Braid (braid.org)?
faqs.answer: Braid is an open protocol and research project that extends HTTP into a state synchronization protocol, adding versioning, subscriptions, patches, and merge-types so web resources can stay synchronized in real time across clients, servers, and peers.

faqs.question: Is Braid a company or a product I can buy?
faqs.answer: No. Braid is a non-commercial, open specification and set of reference implementations maintained by a volunteer community, not a company or a paid product.

faqs.question: Who created Braid?
faqs.answer: Braid is led by Michael Toomim and developed collaboratively through an open working group and the IETF standards process.

faqs.question: How does Braid differ from plain HTTP?
faqs.answer: Plain HTTP transfers a snapshot of a resource on request; Braid adds versioning, real-time subscriptions, incremental patches, and standardized conflict resolution so resources can stay continuously synchronized.

faqs.question: Is Braid the same as GitHub's old 'git braid' submodule tool?
faqs.answer: No. This entry covers the braid.org state-synchronization protocol; 'Braid' has also been used as a name for an unrelated, now-defunct Git submodule tool and for other unaffiliated software projects.

faqs.question: What is Braid-HTTP?
faqs.answer: Braid-HTTP is the core Braid specification formalized as an IETF Internet-Draft that defines how versioning, subscriptions, patches, and merge-types extend the HTTP protocol.

faqs.question: Can I use Braid in production today?
faqs.answer: Reference implementations and demo apps exist and some production deployments have been reported, but Braid remains an evolving draft rather than a finalized standard, so production use requires engineering investment and tolerance for change.

faqs.question: What apps are built on Braid?
faqs.answer: Demonstration and community applications include Peery View, the peer-to-peer chat app Quiet, the collaborative editor Ethersync, and Braidmail, alongside libraries like Braid-Text and Braidfs.

enrichment.short_description: Brainboard is a Paris-based cloud infrastructure management platform, founded in 2019 by Chafik Belhaoues and Jeremy Albinet and backed by Y Combinator, that lets teams visually design multi-cloud architecture and automatically generates production-ready Terraform code from the diagram. Instead of hand-writing HashiCorp Configuration Language, engineers and architects drag and drop resources for AWS, Azure, GCP, OCI, or Scaleway onto a canvas, and Brainboard's engine translates that design into version-controlled, valid Terraform modules in real time. The platform layers AI assistance on top of the visual builder to suggest architecture patterns, flag issues, and help generate diagrams from existing Terraform or reverse-engineer diagrams from already-deployed infrastructure. Beyond diagramming, Brainboard includes an embedded CI/CD engine for running Terraform plan and apply directly from the interface, drift detection that alerts teams when live infrastructure diverges from the designed blueprint, cost estimation via Infracost, and policy and security scanning through integrations with tools like Checkov, tfsec, Terrascan, and OPA. It also offers Git-native workflows with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure DevOps, and role-based access control for team governance.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Brainboard is a cloud infrastructure management platform built around a simple but powerful idea: let engineers and architects design cloud infrastructure visually, then generate correct, production-ready Terraform code automatically from that design, rather than hand-writing HashiCorp Configuration Language from scratch. Founded in 2019 in Paris by Chafik Belhaoues and Jeremy Albinet, and backed by Y Combinator, Brainboard has grown into a multi-cloud platform supporting AWS, Azure, Google Cloud Platform, Oracle Cloud Infrastructure, and Scaleway within a single unified visual workspace. The core workflow starts with a drag-and-drop canvas: users place cloud resources - VPCs, compute instances, databases, load balancers, and so on - onto a diagram, connect them, and configure their properties through the UI. As the diagram takes shape, Brainboard's engine continuously generates the corresponding Terraform code behind the scenes, keeping the visual design and the underlying infrastructure-as-code in sync so that the diagram effectively becomes living documentation rather than a static picture that goes stale. Brainboard has increasingly layered AI on top of this visual foundation. Its AI capabilities can suggest architecture patterns, help flag potential issues in a design, generate a visual diagram directly from an existing Terraform codebase, and even reverse-engineer a diagram from infrastructure that is already deployed in a cloud account, which is useful for teams trying to document and gain visibility into infrastructure that was built ad hoc over time. Beyond the diagram-to-code workflow, Brainboard positions itself as a fuller infrastructure governance platform: it includes an embedded CI/CD engine so teams can run Terraform plan and apply directly from within Brainboard rather than switching to a separate pipeline tool, drift detection that compares live cloud infrastructure against the designed blueprint and alerts teams when they diverge, and cost estimation powered by an Infracost integration so cost implications are visible before changes are applied. On the governance and security side, Brainboard integrates with policy and security scanning tools including Checkov, tfsec, Terrascan, and Open Policy Agent (OPA), and supports native Git workflows across GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure DevOps, along with role-based access control for managing who can view or change infrastructure designs. Brainboard offers a free plan to let individuals and small teams try the core visual-to-Terraform workflow, with paid plans that scale by user count and feature access; publicly available detail on exact paid pricing is limited, with third-party trackers citing a starting price in the neighborhood of 99 dollars per month per user for its entry paid tier, though prospective buyers should confirm current numbers directly with Brainboard's sales team. The platform is best suited to cloud architects, platform engineering teams, and DevOps practitioners who want a visual, governed layer on top of Terraform rather than teams who prefer to write and review all infrastructure code by hand.

long_form_blocks.heading: From Diagram to Terraform: How Brainboard's Core Workflow Works
paragraphs: Brainboard's starting point is a drag-and-drop canvas where users assemble cloud architecture visually - placing compute, storage, networking, and database resources for AWS, Azure, GCP, OCI, or Scaleway and connecting them the way they would in an architecture diagram. The difference from a typical diagramming tool is that every element on the canvas maps to a real, configurable cloud resource, and as the design changes, Brainboard's engine continuously generates the corresponding Terraform code behind the scenes.

This means the visual diagram and the infrastructure-as-code stay in sync automatically, so the diagram can double as accurate, living documentation of the environment rather than a picture that goes stale the moment someone makes a manual change. Brainboard also supports the reverse direction: generating a visual diagram from an existing Terraform codebase, or from infrastructure that's already deployed in a cloud account, which helps teams retroactively document and understand environments that were built up ad hoc.

long_form_blocks.heading: Beyond Diagrams: CI/CD, Drift Detection, Cost and Security
paragraphs: Brainboard goes beyond just generating Terraform code by including an embedded CI/CD engine that lets teams run Terraform plan and apply directly from within the platform, reducing the need to bolt on a separate pipeline tool just to deploy the infrastructure they've designed. Drift detection continuously compares live cloud infrastructure against the intended blueprint and alerts teams when reality has diverged from the design, which is a common and often under-monitored source of configuration risk in growing cloud environments.

On the cost and security side, Brainboard integrates Infracost for cost estimation before changes are applied, and connects to policy and security scanning tools including Checkov, tfsec, Terrascan, and Open Policy Agent (OPA) so misconfigurations and policy violations can be caught before they reach production. Combined with native Git integration across GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, and Azure DevOps, and role-based access control, Brainboard positions itself less as a pure diagramming tool and more as a governance layer for infrastructure-as-code teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who Brainboard Is Built For
paragraphs: Brainboard is aimed squarely at cloud architects, platform engineering teams, and DevOps practitioners who want the clarity of visual architecture design combined with the rigor of version-controlled, production-grade Terraform, without every team member needing deep HCL fluency to contribute to or review infrastructure changes. Its multi-cloud support in a single workspace also appeals to organizations managing resources across more than one cloud provider.

Teams that prefer a purely code-first workflow, or that are fully committed to a different IaC toolchain, may find the visual abstraction layer less necessary. Brainboard offers a free plan for smaller teams and individuals to evaluate the core workflow, with paid plans priced per user for teams that need the CI/CD, drift detection, cost, and governance features at scale; exact current pricing for paid tiers is best confirmed directly with Brainboard, since detailed figures are not fully published on its own site.

enrichment.short_description: There are two unrelated companies named Braintrust. This profile covers Braintrust (braintrust.dev), an AI observability and evaluation platform founded in 2023 by Ankur Goyal, previously founder of Impira (acquired by Figma), and headquartered in San Francisco. Braintrust helps teams building LLM-powered products and AI agents trace requests in production, run offline evaluations against datasets, score outputs with LLM graders, code, or human review, and catch quality regressions before they reach users. Its eval-first philosophy starts from defining what a good output looks like and measuring against that, rather than only capturing logs and searching after the fact. The platform includes Loop, an AI assistant that analyzes production traces and recommends custom scorers, and Brainstore, a purpose-built data store for querying millions of nested agent traces quickly. In February 2026 the company raised an 80 million dollar Series B led by ICONIQ at an 800 million dollar valuation. Note: this is a different company from Braintrust (usebraintrust.com), a blockchain-based, token-incentivized freelance talent marketplace founded in San Jose in 2018 by Adam Jackson, Gabriel Luna-Ostaseski, and Brian Flynn - do not confuse the two.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Because the name 'Braintrust' is shared by two unrelated companies, it's worth being precise about which one this profile describes. Braintrust (braintrust.dev) is an AI observability and evaluation platform founded in 2023 by Ankur Goyal, who previously founded Impira, a data-extraction and document-management company that was acquired by Figma, where Goyal went on to lead the AI team before starting Braintrust. Headquartered in San Francisco, Braintrust exists to solve a problem that became urgent as teams shipped more LLM-powered products and AI agents into production: traditional software observability tools weren't built for the non-deterministic, quality-sensitive nature of AI outputs, and traditional testing frameworks weren't built for probabilistic model behavior. Braintrust's approach is described as 'eval-first' - rather than starting from 'capture everything and search later,' the platform encourages teams to define what a good output looks like first, build a dataset and scoring criteria around that definition, and then continuously measure new prompts, models, and code changes against it. In practice this plays out across a few connected capabilities. Tracing lets teams inspect every request, tool call, and step of an AI agent's reasoning in production, searching across millions of logs and tracking latency, cost, and quality in real time. The evaluation framework lets teams run experiments against real datasets, compare prompts and models side by side, and score outputs using LLM-based graders, code-based scorers, or human reviewers, which is particularly useful for catching regressions before a prompt or model change reaches end users. Two more specialized capabilities round out the platform: Loop, an AI assistant for evaluation development that can take a natural-language description of a failure a team wants to investigate and use it to analyze production traces, generate a relevant evaluation dataset, and recommend custom scorers; and Brainstore, a data store purpose-built for AI workloads, designed to support fast queries across millions of agent traces, including large nested trace trees and high-cardinality observability data that general-purpose logging systems struggle with. Braintrust is explicitly framework- and model-agnostic, offering SDKs for Python, TypeScript, Go, Ruby, and C#, and it's designed to slot into an existing AI stack without requiring a rewrite. Its customer base includes well-known technology companies such as Notion, Stripe, Vercel, Airtable, Instacart, Zapier, Ramp, Dropbox, Cloudflare, and BILL, with Notion publicly crediting the platform for a significant improvement in how quickly it can find and fix AI quality issues. The company's momentum has been reflected in its funding: in February 2026, Braintrust raised an 80 million dollar Series B led by ICONIQ, with participation from Andreessen Horowitz, Greylock, and Elad Gil, at an 800 million dollar valuation. It's worth being clear about the other 'Braintrust': usebraintrust.com is a completely different, unrelated company - a blockchain-based, token-incentivized freelance talent marketplace founded in San Jose in 2018 by Adam Jackson, Gabriel Luna-Ostaseski, and Brian Flynn, which connects vetted knowledge workers with enterprise clients like Nestle, Porsche, and Nike, charges a flat percent project fee, and uses its BTRST token to distribute governance and rewards across the network. Anyone researching 'Braintrust' for hiring/marketplace purposes should look at usebraintrust.com, while anyone researching 'Braintrust' for AI evaluation, observability, or LLM tooling purposes should look at braintrust.dev, which is the company profiled here.

long_form_blocks.heading: Braintrust's Eval-First Approach to AI Quality
paragraphs: Most software observability tools were built around a 'log everything, search later' model that works well for deterministic systems where a bug either exists or doesn't. LLM-powered products are different: the same prompt can produce subtly different, sometimes wrong, sometimes brilliant outputs, and 'quality' is often a matter of degree rather than pass/fail. Braintrust's founding premise is that teams building AI products need to start from defining what a good output looks like - through datasets, scoring criteria, and explicit test cases - and then measure every change against that definition, rather than discovering quality problems only after users complain.

This eval-first philosophy shapes the whole product: the evaluation framework lets teams run experiments against real datasets, compare prompts and models side by side, and score results using LLM-based graders, code-based scorers, or human review, so that a prompt or model change can be validated before it ships rather than debugged after the fact in production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Tracing, Loop, and Brainstore: How the Platform Fits Together
paragraphs: Evaluation alone isn't enough once an AI product is live, so Braintrust also provides production tracing: the ability to inspect every request, tool call, and reasoning step an AI agent takes, search across millions of logs, and track latency, cost, and quality metrics in real time. This closes the loop between what a team tested before shipping and what's actually happening with real users.

Two more specialized pieces extend this further. Loop is Braintrust's AI assistant for evaluation development - a team can describe, in natural language, a failure mode they want to investigate, and Loop will analyze the relevant production traces, generate an evaluation dataset around that failure, and recommend custom scorers to catch it going forward. Brainstore is the underlying data infrastructure making all of this fast at scale: a data store purpose-built for AI workloads, designed to support quick queries across millions of nested agent traces and high-cardinality observability data, which general-purpose logging databases are not well suited to handle.

long_form_blocks.heading: A Note on the Other Braintrust
paragraphs: Anyone researching 'Braintrust' should be aware that the name is shared by two unconnected companies. Braintrust (braintrust.dev), covered in this profile, is the San Francisco-based AI observability and evaluation platform founded by Ankur Goyal in 2023. Braintrust (usebraintrust.com) is a separate, blockchain-based freelance talent marketplace founded in San Jose in 2018 by Adam Jackson, Gabriel Luna-Ostaseski, and Brian Flynn, which connects vetted technical and design freelancers with enterprise clients and uses a BTRST token to distribute network governance and incentives to its community of contributors.

The two companies operate in entirely different markets - one is developer infrastructure for AI quality, the other is a hiring and freelance marketplace - and share no corporate relationship. Given how similar the names and even some of the branding language ('Braintrust,' talent, quality) can look at a glance, it's worth double-checking which Braintrust a source, article, or job posting is actually referring to before drawing conclusions.

enrichment.short_description: Branch (branch.io), legally Branch Metrics, Inc., is a mobile measurement partner (MMP) and deep-linking platform founded in April 2014 in Palo Alto, California, by Alex Austin, Mike Molinet, Mada Seghete, and Dmitri Gaskin. It is not affiliated with Branch Insurance, Branch Furniture, or Branch Messenger - it is specifically the mobile attribution and linking company used across app marketing stacks. Branch's deep links route users from ads, email, SMS, social, and the mobile web directly into the right in-app screen, handling thousands of device and platform edge cases so links don't break across iOS, Android, and app-not-installed states. On top of linking, Branch provides privacy-safe, cross-channel attribution that ties ad spend and organic activity to in-app outcomes, unifying paid, owned, and organic performance data across more than 80 partner integrations, including Meta, Google, TikTok, Snap, Amplitude, and Braze. The platform reports serving mobile measurement and linking for over 100,000 apps and roughly 3.5 billion users worldwide, and it holds ISO 27001 certification. Branch has raised roughly 666 million dollars in venture funding, including a 300 million dollar Series F in February 2022 led by New Enterprise Associates that valued the company at 4 billion dollars, with additional backing from Founders Fund, Playground Global, and Pear VC.

enrichment.seo_title: Branch.io Review: Mobile Deep Linking and Attribution Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Branch (Branch.io) is a leading mobile measurement and deep linking platform. See how it works, its pricing tiers, and how it differs from Branch Insurance or Branch Furniture.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Branch, commonly known by its URL branch.io and legally registered as Branch Metrics, Inc., is one of the best-known names in mobile marketing infrastructure, alongside competitors like AppsFlyer and Adjust. It's worth flagging up front that 'Branch' is used by several unrelated companies - Branch Insurance, Branch Furniture, and Branch Messenger are all different businesses - and this profile is specifically about Branch Metrics, the mobile measurement and deep-linking company. Branch was founded in April 2014 by Alex Austin, Mike Molinet, Mada Seghete, and Dmitri Gaskin, reportedly after the founders ran into the problem of broken and unreliable app links while building their own product. The company's foundational technology is deep linking: instead of a marketing link simply opening an app to its home screen (or failing entirely if the app isn't installed), Branch's links route users to the exact in-app content that matches what they clicked, whether that click came from an email, an SMS, a social post, a paid ad, or the mobile web. Branch describes handling more than 6,000 device, platform, and install-state edge cases so that these deep links behave consistently across iOS, Android, and scenarios where the app isn't yet installed (deferred deep linking), which is a much harder engineering problem than it sounds given the fragmentation of mobile operating systems, browsers, and app stores. On top of linking, Branch operates as a full mobile measurement partner (MMP), providing attribution that connects marketing spend and organic activity to in-app outcomes across more than 80 integrated partners, including major ad platforms like Meta, Google, TikTok, and Snap, as well as analytics and engagement tools like Amplitude and Braze. Branch markets this as privacy-safe measurement, an important positioning given how significantly Apple's App Tracking Transparency and the shift toward SKAdNetwork (SKAN) have changed what attribution data is available on iOS; Branch's product lineup includes advanced SKAN modeling as part of its higher-tier offerings. The company reports scale metrics of over 100,000 customer apps and roughly 3.5 billion users measured worldwide, and holds ISO 27001 certification, which matters for enterprise buyers evaluating data-handling practices. Branch's pricing is structured around three named tiers - Basics, Essentials, and Enterprise - with capabilities like multi-touch attribution and unlimited partner connections unlocked in Essentials, and CTV/OTT attribution, LTV prediction, advanced SKAN modeling, and unlimited API access reserved for Enterprise. However, Branch does not publish specific list prices on its website; pricing is quoted through a sales process, and third-party sources describing real-world costs vary widely, from smaller reported starting figures up to tens of thousands of dollars annually for mid-market apps and well into six figures annually for the largest enterprise deployments with high monthly active user counts. Branch has raised substantial venture funding over its history - approximately 666 million dollars in total, including a 300 million dollar Series F round in February 2022 led by New Enterprise Associates that valued the company at 4 billion dollars, with additional investors including Founders Fund, Playground Global, and Pear VC - reflecting its position as one of the largest independent players in mobile attribution and linking.

long_form_blocks.heading: Deep Linking: Branch's Foundational Technology
paragraphs: Branch's core product problem is deceptively simple to describe and genuinely hard to solve well: when someone clicks a marketing link meant to open an app, that link needs to take them to the exact right piece of content inside the app, whether they already have the app installed, need to install it first, are on iOS or Android, or are browsing from a desktop. A naive implementation either breaks (dead link, generic app-store redirect) or loses the context of what the user actually clicked on.

Branch's deep-linking infrastructure is built to handle this reliably across what the company describes as more than 6,000 device, browser, and platform edge cases, including deferred deep linking - remembering a user's intended destination through an app install so they land on the right screen the first time they open the app, not just the home screen. This reliability is the foundation the rest of Branch's attribution and marketing products are built on top of.

long_form_blocks.heading: Attribution in a Privacy-First Mobile Ecosystem
paragraphs: As a mobile measurement partner (MMP), Branch's second core function is tying marketing activity - paid ads, email, SMS, social, organic search - to what actually happens inside an app after a user arrives. This is the layer that lets marketing teams answer questions like which channel drove the highest-value users, not just which channel drove the most installs. Branch integrates with more than 80 partners across the ad and MarTech ecosystem, including Meta, Google, TikTok, Snap, Amplitude, and Braze, to pull this picture together.

The mobile attribution landscape has been reshaped over the past several years by privacy changes, particularly Apple's App Tracking Transparency framework and the resulting shift toward Apple's SKAdNetwork (SKAN) as a privacy-preserving measurement mechanism. Branch has built advanced SKAN modeling into its higher-tier product offering as part of adapting to this shift, alongside broader claims of privacy-safe measurement across its attribution stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Structure and Market Position
paragraphs: Branch organizes its pricing around three named tiers: Basics, an entry tier with a free trial and foundational measurement and dashboard capabilities; Essentials, which adds multi-touch attribution, unlimited partner connections, and fraud detection; and Enterprise, which adds CTV/OTT attribution, LTV prediction, advanced SKAN modeling, and unlimited API access. Branch does not publish specific list prices for these tiers on its website, and third-party sources describing typical real-world costs vary considerably, so prospective buyers should treat any specific number found outside of Branch's own sales process with caution and get a direct quote.

With roughly 666 million dollars in total venture funding, a 4 billion dollar valuation from its February 2022 Series F, and backers including New Enterprise Associates, Founders Fund, Playground Global, and Pear VC, Branch is one of the largest and best-capitalized independent companies in the mobile measurement and deep-linking category, competing most directly with AppsFlyer and Adjust for enterprise mobile marketing and growth teams.

enrichment.short_description: BreakTimer is a free, open-source, cross-platform desktop application that helps computer users avoid eye strain and repetitive strain injury (RSI) by prompting periodic breaks throughout the workday. Built with Electron by developer Tom James Watson and maintained on GitHub under the GPL-3.0 license, BreakTimer runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, with Linux installers available as Snap, AppImage, .deb, and .rpm packages. Users can configure how often breaks occur, how long they last, and the working-hours window during which reminders are active, and can choose between gentle notification alerts or fullscreen break windows that take over the screen to enforce a real pause. The app includes intelligent break-reset detection, so idle time away from the keyboard counts toward rest rather than triggering unnecessary reminders. As a community-maintained open-source project rather than a company, BreakTimer has no pricing, subscription, or account system: it is downloaded and run locally with all break logic staying on-device. The project has around 1,500 GitHub stars and 135 forks, with active releases and an open call from the maintainer for more contributors.

enrichment.seo_title: BreakTimer Review: Free Open-Source Break Reminder App
enrichment.seo_meta_description: BreakTimer is a free, open-source desktop app that reminds you to take breaks and avoid eye strain and RSI. See its features, platforms, and how it compares to alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: BreakTimer is a small, focused, open-source desktop utility built to solve one specific problem: people who spend long hours at a computer often forget to take breaks, leading to eye strain and repetitive strain injury (RSI) over time. Rather than being a broad wellness suite, BreakTimer does one job - reminding users to step away from their screen on a schedule they control - and does it as a free, privacy-friendly, locally-run application rather than a cloud-connected subscription product. The app is built with Electron, which is why it runs consistently across Windows, macOS, and Linux; Linux users in particular have several installer options, including Snap, AppImage, .deb, and .rpm packages, reflecting the diversity of Linux distributions the maintainer supports. The core configuration options are straightforward: users set how frequently breaks should occur, how long each break should last, and a working-hours window so that reminders only fire during the times someone is actually expected to be working, rather than interrupting evenings or weekends. For the reminder itself, users can choose between two notification styles - a lighter-touch alert popup that can be dismissed, or a fullscreen break window that takes over the entire screen, which is useful for anyone who tends to ignore softer nudges and wants the app to actually enforce a pause. A more subtle but useful feature is intelligent break-reset detection: if a user steps away from their computer and it goes idle for a meaningful stretch of time, BreakTimer recognizes that as rest and adjusts its countdown accordingly, rather than demanding a formal 'break' immediately after someone returns from a coffee run they never got credit for. BreakTimer is maintained by developer Tom James Watson and is released under the GPL-3.0 open-source license, meaning the source code is publicly available on GitHub for anyone to inspect, audit, or modify. This matters for privacy-conscious users because it means there's no telemetry or cloud account required by design - the app runs entirely on the user's own machine, and all scheduling logic stays local. There is no company behind BreakTimer, no pricing tiers, and no premium upsell; it is entirely free, and the project depends on volunteer maintenance and community contributions, with the maintainer explicitly welcoming outside contributors to help extend and support the project going forward. As of mid-2026 the GitHub repository shows continued activity, with roughly 1,500 stars, 135 forks, and a recent 2.0.3 release, indicating the project remains actively used and maintained rather than abandoned. Because it's a single-purpose desktop tool, BreakTimer doesn't offer team or enterprise features such as centrally managed break policies across an organization, nor does it sync settings or history across multiple devices - each installation is independent. For an individual user, freelancer, or remote worker looking for a simple, free, and trustworthy way to build regular break habits into a long computer session, BreakTimer's narrow focus is arguably its main strength: it does the one thing it promises to do, without asking for money, an account, or your data in return.

long_form_blocks.heading: Why a Simple Break Reminder App Still Matters
paragraphs: Long, uninterrupted computer sessions are a well-documented contributor to eye strain and repetitive strain injury, yet most people are bad at self-regulating breaks without an external prompt - especially during focused, deadline-driven work. BreakTimer exists to close that gap with the simplest possible intervention: a configurable, locally-run reminder that interrupts a work session on a schedule the user defines, rather than relying on willpower or memory.

Unlike broader wellness or productivity suites that bundle break reminders alongside habit tracking, hydration logging, or posture monitoring, BreakTimer stays narrowly focused on the reminder itself. That narrow scope is part of why it remains lightweight and easy to reason about as an open-source project, and why a solo maintainer has been able to sustain it over multiple years of releases.

long_form_blocks.heading: How BreakTimer's Reminder System Works
paragraphs: Users configure three core settings: break frequency (how often a break should be prompted), break duration (how long the break should last), and a working-hours window so the app only reminds during the hours someone is actually expected to be at their desk. On top of scheduling, users choose how forcefully BreakTimer should intervene - a dismissible notification alert for a gentler nudge, or a fullscreen break window that takes over the screen entirely, which is designed for users who know they'll ignore a soft reminder if given the option.

A quieter but meaningful feature is BreakTimer's break-reset detection: the app can tell when a user has stepped away from the keyboard and treat that idle time as an actual rest period, adjusting its internal countdown so people aren't prompted for a formal break moments after they've already taken an informal one. Combined with customizable break messages, this makes the tool feel less like a rigid timer and more like an assistant that's roughly aware of how someone is actually using their computer.

long_form_blocks.heading: Free, Open Source, and Locally Run
paragraphs: BreakTimer is maintained by developer Tom James Watson and released under the GPL-3.0 license, with its full source code publicly available on GitHub. There is no company, subscription, or account system involved: the app is downloaded and installed like any other desktop application, and all break-scheduling logic runs locally on the user's machine, with no cloud dependency required for the core functionality.

This open-source, local-first model has two practical implications. First, it's genuinely free with no premium tier, ads, or upsell - the entire feature set described here is available to every user. Second, because the code is open and auditable, privacy-conscious users can verify there's nothing tracking their activity behind the scenes. The tradeoff is that, as a volunteer-maintained project, BreakTimer's roadmap and support depend on community bandwidth rather than a dedicated support team, and it doesn't include team or enterprise features like centrally managed break policies across an organization.
